coredata.dc:title: Design of a bioelectronic tongue for glucose monitoring using zinc oxide nanofibers and graphene derivatives
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mercante, Luiza A.
Andre, Rafaela S.
Facure, Murilo H.M.
Fugikawa-Santos, Lucas
Correa, Daniel S.
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring glucose levels is critical for diabetes management and might be a key step in the development of individualized treatment strategies. In this scenario, tracking salivary glucose has been recognized as a promising strategy due to its merits of ease sampling and non-invasive nature. In this paper, we report on the development of an electrical impedance-based biosensor array to distinguish glucose at different concentrations in saliva. The enzymatic biosensors were made of gold interdigitated electrodes coated with pristine electrospun zinc oxide nanofibers (NFZ) and NFZ combined with graphene-based nanomaterials (i.e., reduced graphene oxide - rGO and graphene quantum dots - GQDs), on which a layer of glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme was adsorbed. Electrical impedance measurements indicate that the NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx platforms presented good linear relationship with glucose concentration in the range of 0.1 to 6 mM. The highest sensitivity was reached for NFZ-rGO@GOx with a detection limit (LOD) of 14 μM, while the LOD was 32 μM for NFZ-GQDs@GOx. Both biosensors were also capable of detecting glucose in artificial saliva using aliquots of 10 μL, with recovery between 87.3 and 106.8%. Furthermore, the three sensing units (NFZ@GOx, NFZ-rGO@GOx and NFZ-GQDs@GOx) were employed to build a bioelectronic tongue. Using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) technique to project the electrical impedance data of all sensing units allowed the discrimination of the different glucose concentrations and interferents. This study reveals the applicability of the developed bioelectronic tongue as non-invasive glucose sensors, which approach could also be pottentially adapted to detect other disease biomarkers present in saliva.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospinning
Electrospun nanofibers
Reduced graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Glucose detection
Biosensor array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and ZnO-GQDs nanofibers (NFZ-GQDs)
Synthesis of rGO and ZnO-rGO nanofibers (NFZ-rGO)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo (FAPESP) (2016/23793-4, 2017/10582-8, 2017/12174-4, 2018/22214-6), Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), MCTI-SisNano (CNPq/402.287/2013-4), and Rede Agronano (EMBRAPA) from Brazil. The authors also thank the Laboratory of Structural Characterization (LCE/DEMa/UFSCar) for the microscopy facilities and Dr.Valmor Mastelaro (IFSC-USP) for the XPS measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FEG-SEM, (b) TEM and (c) high-resolution lattice images of NFZ. (d) EDS elemental mapping images of Zn and O.
(a) XRD patterns and XPS data: (b) high-resolution Zn 2p, (c) high-resolution O 1s spectra of NFZ, NFZ-GQDs and NFZ-rGO and (d) high-resolution C1s spectra of NFZ-GQDs and NFZ-rGO.
Comparison of electrical resistance versus frequency of NFZ, NFZ-GQDs and NFZ-rGO. Measurements were carried out using AC voltage of 50 mV, in the frequency range of 10 Hz up to 1 MHz.

Nyquist plots of (a) NFZ and NFZ@GOx, (b) NFZ-GQDs and NFZ-GQDs@GOx and (c) NFZ-rGO and NFZ-rGO@GOx. (d) Equivalent circuits of (i) NFZ@GOx and (ii) NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx, which circuit component parameters are displayed in Table 1.

Calibration curves of glucose detection for (a) NFZ-GQDs@GOx and (b) NFZ-rGO@GOx obtained by plotting the R2 values as a function of glucose concentration in the range of 0.1 to 6 mM. The insets show the Nyquist plots for different glucose concentrations.

PCA plot obtained with electrical impedance data as a function of frequency for each of the three sensing units exposed to (a) different glucose concentrations. In panel (b) two clusters were formed on the projection, with glucose samples located on the blue region and the interferents (AA: ascorbic acid and inorganic salts: CaCl2, KCl and NaCl) on the orange region. Each point represents an impedance spectrum for each glucose concentration. The relative contributions of PC1 (x-axis) and PC2 (y-axis) were calculated to be (a) 83.7 and 10.1% and (b) 53.3% and 25.75, respectively. The silhouette coefficients (S) are 0.73.
Equivalent circuit component parameters used to fit the Nyquist plots of electrodes modified with glucose oxidase.
Comparison of the performances for the various types of glucose biosensors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CNFs: carbon nanofibers; GO – graphene oxide; PANI: polyaniline; PPD: 1,10-phenanthroline-5,6-dione, PPCA: poly(pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid); AuNP: gold nanoparticle; TA: thioctic acid; CNC: cellulose nanocrystal; APTES: 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of glucose in artificial saliva samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Design of a bioelectronic tongue for glucose monitoring using zinc oxide nanofibers and graphene derivatives

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Brazilian Agricultural Research Corporation: Empresa Brasileira de Pesquisa Agropecuaria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring glucose levels is critical for diabetes management and might be a key step in the development of individualized treatment strategies. In this scenario, tracking salivary glucose has been recognized as a promising strategy due to its merits of ease sampling and non-invasive nature. In this paper, we report on the development of an electrical impedance-based biosensor array to distinguish glucose at different concentrations in saliva. The enzymatic biosensors were made of gold interdigitated electrodes coated with pristine electrospun zinc oxide nanofibers (NFZ) and NFZ combined with graphene-based nanomaterials (i.e., reduced graphene oxide - rGO and graphene quantum dots - GQDs), on which a layer of glucose oxidase (GOx) enzyme was adsorbed. Electrical impedance measurements indicate that the NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx platforms presented good linear relationship with glucose concentration in the range of 0.1 to 6 mM. The highest sensitivity was reached for NFZ-rGO@GOx with a detection limit (LOD) of 14 μM, while the LOD was 32 μM for NFZ-GQDs@GOx. Both biosensors were also capable of detecting glucose in artificial saliva using aliquots of 10 μL, with recovery between 87.3 and 106.8%. Furthermore, the three sensing units (NFZ@GOx, NFZ-rGO@GOx and NFZ-GQDs@GOx) were employed to build a bioelectronic tongue. Using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) technique to project the electrical impedance data of all sensing units allowed the discrimination of the different glucose concentrations and interferents. This study reveals the applicability of the developed bioelectronic tongue as non-invasive glucose sensors, which approach could also be pottentially adapted to detect other disease biomarkers present in saliva.

ce:section.ce:para: Accurate and continuous monitoring of metabolites in human body fluids is of critical importance for health monitoring and management [1–3]. For instance, early detection of glucose level is highly important for proper diabetic diagnosis [4,5]. In recent years, saliva monitoring has been introduced as an attractive alternative to conventional glucose assays owing to its ease and non-invasive nature of collection [6–9]. In order to advance the performance of salivary glucose biosensors, new sensing materials and data acquisition tools are needed for the precise and rapid non-invasive monitoring of patients with diabetes worldwide.

In this scenario, nanomaterials appear as promising alternatives, once they combine high surface area and tunable surface chemistry with various surface modifications to meet the requirements for sensing applications. All the combined features facilitate the design of portable, fast, highly sensitive and selective biosensors for non-invasive detection [1,10–13]. Among the many nanostructures used for biosensors design, electrospun nanofibers have provided high performance due to their large surface area, high porosity, ease of functionalization and easy fabrication [61][14,15]. In addition, these nanofibers are advantageous because they form three-dimensional platforms that offer a biocompatible surface, which assists in the active layer immobilization with suitable biological activity [15–17][61]. The possibility to buildup multifunctional nanostructures by combining different types of nanofibers with a wide range of distinct nanomaterials could also enhance the biosensor performance through synergistic effects [18–20]. All these features open the possibility to develop a wide variety of nanofibers-based biosensors for the detection of biomarkers using distinctive transduction methods. However, until now most of the biosensors based on electrospun nanofibers reported in the literature are based on electrochemical transducers [21,22][61]. Surprisingly, we were unable to find in the literature examples of ceramic electrospun nanofibers employed as sensing layers in electrical impedance biosensors for glucose monitoring.

Electrical impedance spectroscopy is a powerful method for biosensing since the materials’ electrical properties are highly dependent on their interaction with the environment/analyte [23,24]. In addition, this technique has also been proven to be useful in the monitoring/detection of multiple analytes in complex media by employing multiple sensing units combined with data processing tools, as it is the case of (bio)electronic tongues, which provide a matrix of information for global selectivity [23,25]. Briefly, a bioelectronic tongue combines an array of cross-sensitive biosensors capable of providing a wide and complete response toward the analyzed species. Besides, it works as a modeling tool able to interpret and extract meaningful data from complex readings, regardless of the sensing mechanisms involved [28–34]. Moreover, the data processing stage may offset any matrix effect and allows the resolution of the interferences [26], representing a powerfull tool to overcome selectivity issues in complex media like biological fluids.

In this paper, we report on novel biosensors architectures fabricated with electrospun ZnO nanofibers combined with graphene-based nanomaterials and glucose oxidase (GOx). The choice of ZnO was motivated by its biocompatible nature, strong adsorption ability due to high isoelectric point (∼ 9.5), good electrical conductivity, chemical stability, resistance against corrosion and oxidation, which features are advantageous for biosensing devices [27,28]. Graphene-based nanomaterials have also demonstrated enhanced electrical conductivity, chemical stability, biocompatible property and facilitate interplays between element detection and target biomolecules [1,11,29,30]. Specifically, we have functionalized zinc oxide electrospun nanofibers (NFZ) with two different types of graphene-based nanomaterials, namely graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). The nanomaterials were characterized regarding their chemical composition and electrical properties, and then functionalized with GOx for the detection of glucose at different levels using electrical impedance spectroscopy. Furthermore, the three sensing units, namely NFZ@GOx, NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx, were assembled into an array to build an enzymatic bioelectronic tongue. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the development of a bioelectronic tongue based on electrospun ceramic nanofibers. By correlating electrical impedance data using PCA method, the sensor array has shownability to clearly distinguish different glucose concentrations and interferents in an expedite way.

Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2•6H2O), Poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP, Mw 1300,000), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and glucose oxidase (GOx) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Glucose, ascorbic acid (AA), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4) and dibasic potassium phosphate (K2HPO4) were obtained from Synth Chemical (São Paulo, Brazil). All the chemical reagents were of analytical-grade and used without any further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) 0.1 mol L− 1 at pH 7.0 was prepared with Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. The glucose stock solution was prepared in PBS and stored in concentrations of 10 mol L–1. Artificial saliva was purchased from a local pharmacy and is composed of inorganic salts and xylitol in water.

The NFZ was prepared following similar procedure described in our previous work [31]. Briefly, 0.4 g PVP and 0.4 g Zn(NO3)2•6H2O were dispersed in 3 mL of DMF under vigorous stirring for 6 h at room temperature. The nanofibers were electrospun using the following experimental parameters: feed rate of 0.3 mL h− 1, electric voltage of 20 kV, working distance of 6 cm between the syringe and the metallic collector, and a steel needle of diameter of 0.8 mm. In order to remove the organic phase and solvent and promote the zinc oxide crystallization, the as-spun nanofibers were calcinated at 500 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and ZnO-GQDs nanofibers (NFZ-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthetized following the procedure described previously by Sun et al. [32]. To produce the NFZ-GQDs, 0.4 g PVP and 0.4 g Zn(NO3)2•6H2O were first dispersed in 2 mL of DMF, then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6 h. After complete dispersion, 1 mL of the as-synthesized GQDs in DMF was added under vigorous stirring. The resulted homogenous dispersion was electrospun employing the same parameters as for the neat NFZ. The final NFZ-GQDs nanofibers were prepared by submitting the electrospun precursor fibers at a high-temperature treatment, as described before.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rGO and ZnO-rGO nanofibers (NFZ-rGO)
ce:section.ce:para: Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was prepared by an eco-friendly modified Hummers method followed by a chemical reduction with sodium citrate, according to our previous works [33]. The hybrid nanofibers (NFZ-rGO) were then obtained by ultrasonic dispersion of 2 mg NFZ in a rGO aqueous suspension (0.1 mg mL−1) for 5 min. The hybrid material was collected and dried at room temperature.

The modified electrodes were prepared by drop casting 10 μL of each nanomaterial solution (1 mg mL−1) onto gold interdigitated electrodes (IDEs) and leaving them to dry overnight at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C). The IDEs were fabricated by photolithography manufactured onto glass substrate using the facility available at the microfabrication laboratory (LMF/LNNano-LNLS, Campinas-Brazil). Each IDE was composed of 50 pairs of fingers, with width and gap between fingers of 10 μm.

Prior to the electrode modification, bare IDEs were subjected to impedance measurements in PBS (0.1 mol L− 1, pH 7.0) to check the reproducibility of their responses. Such procedure was carried out to ensure that changes in the electrical responses of each modified sensing unit were due to the variation of chemical composition of the nanocomposite films, instead of differences among the IDEs themselves.

ZnO and graphene-based nanomaterials are known to be suitable for the adsorption of proteins/enzymes [27,28]. The adsorption immobilization procedure is advantageous once it is a single step reagent process that can be generally appropriate to any enzyme [61]. Therefore, GOx was immobilized on the modified IDEs by drop-casting 5 μL of GOx solution (1 mg mL−1 in PBS). The NFZ/GOx, NFZ-GQDs/GOx, NFZ-rGO/GOx modified IDEs were kept at 4 °C overnight and then washed twice with PBS to remove any unadsorbed enzyme from the electrodes surface.

The surface morphology and microstructure of the nanofibers were evaluated by field-emission gun scanning electron microscopy (FEG-SEM, JEOL-JSM 6701F). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a FEI Tecnai G2F20 microscope operated with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

Phase identification was performed by X-ray diffraction technique (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154059 nm), 30 kV and 30 mA (Shimadzu XRD-6000). For the measurements, the samples were placed in a glass sample holder.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by an ESCALAB-MKII spectrometer (UK) with Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source for excitation. The binding energies were evaluated using C1s spectrum (284.6 eV) as the reference with an accuracy of ± 0.1 eV High-resolution spectra were deconvoluted into Lorentzian–Gaussian peaks using a Shirley background.

Electrical characterization of the sensing units was performed by AC impedance spectroscopy using a Solartron 1260 controlled by ZPlot software with a scan rate of 5 pt/dec acquisition, an AC input of 50 mV and the frequency range from 10 Hz to 1 MHz. The IDEs were connected to the Solartron by an edge connector (2 × 12 pin, Metaltex).

The sensing performance of the three sensing units was evaluated by electrical impedance measurements over different concentrations of glucose (0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 mM) starting from the lowest to the highest concentration, keeping the frequency range from 10 up to 1 MHz at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C). The sensing units were left in contact with each solution 10 min before data acquisition to reach a stable electrical response, and three consecutive measurements were performed with each sensing unit. For real sample analysis, the artificial saliva was diluted 5-fold in PBS buffer before the addition of glucose.

An impedimetric bioelectronic tongue composed by the three sensing units (NFZ@GOx, NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx) was tested regarding its capability in discriminating different concentrations of glucose and its potential interferents. For that purpose, the impedance data was analyzed by multidimensional information visualization techniques using the PEx-Sensors software [34,35] and the PCA projection technique was employed to reduce the data dimensionality, transforming them into 2D maps. The projection technique first reduces the dimension of each spectrum by Fastmap and PCA algorithms, and then calculates dissimilarities between samples through Euclidean distances. The software allows the use of the whole impedance spectra for all analysis conditions instead of searching and selecting only the most relevant frequency as it is traditionally made in data analysis of sensors.

The morphology of the NFZ sample was investigated by FEG-SEM and TEM. The FEG-SEM image (Fig. 1 a) revealed the formation of randomly oriented nanofibers with a mean diameter of 145 nm and lengths of several micrometers. The TEM image depicted in Fig. 1b clearly shows that the nanofibers are actually composed of compactly packed nanoparticles. Such structure provides the NFZ a highly porous morphology, delivering a larger number of catalytic sites than a dense morphology, which is favorable for sensing applications. Lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.247 nm, corresponding to (002) plane of hexagonal ZnO, could be further observed from high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c). The elemental mapping of NFZ shown in Fig. 1d confirms that Zn (green) e O (red) elements are distributed uniformly throughout the whole region of the nanofibers.

In an attempt to improve the electrical properties of the ceramic nanofibers, two nanocomposites were prepared by combining NFZ with graphene-based nanomaterials. For that purpose, GQDs and rGO were chosen due to their ability to form hybrid materials with outstanding sensing properties [1,29]. The formation of the hybrid nanomaterials was first confirmed by XRD measurements. Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of NFZ, NFZ-GQDs and NFZ-rGO. All samples display a series of diffraction peaks at 2θ = 31.8°, 34.4°, 36.2°, 47.5°, 56.6°, 62.9° and 67.8°, which can be attributed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) planes of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO, respectively (JCPDS no 36–1451) [36]. The XRD pattern of the NFZ-GQDs displays a characteristic broad peak centered at 2θ = 23.7°, corresponding to the (002) plane of carbon materials. Compared to NFZ, the NFZ-rGO also exhibits a broad diffraction peak at around 25°, suggesting that rGO, comprised mainly of few- or single-layer sheets, was successfully assembled on NFZ [37].

To further characterize the as-developed nanocomposites, their chemical surface composition and element analysis were investigated by XPS. Fig. 2b shows the corresponding high-resolution Zn 2p scans of NFZ and its nanocomposites. All the samples exhibited the typical two peaks corresponding to the Zn2+ state, confirming the presence of ZnO in all materials. For NFZ, these two peaks are centered at 1021.7 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and at 1044.8 eV (Zn 2p1/2) and the observed spin-orbit splitting of Zn 2p (difference between Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2) is about 23 eV. In comparison with pristine ZnO nanofibers, the binding energy of Zn 2p3/2 peak for NFZ-GQDs is shifted to lower binding energy (∼ 1 eV), which could be attributed to the strong interfacial interactions between ZnO and GQDs [38]. The absence of an observable shift in the Zn 2p3/2 peak from NFZ-rGO indicates the effect of the chemical environment on the rGO-modified ZnO nanofibers was negligible [39]. The different behaviors might be related to the different approaches used to prepare the nanocomposites, since the NFZ-GQDs was prepared by a one-step process and the NFZ-rGO was obtained by the post-modification of the nanofibers. In Fig. 2c, the O 1s spectrum of NFZ can be seen to entail two subpeaks with binding energies at 530.1 and 531.8 eV, which are attributed to O2– ion in the ZnO lattice and to the surface-adsorbed OH group, respectively [40]. For the nanocomposites, an additional peak at 533.4 and 532.3 was observed for NFZ-GDs and NFZ-rGO, respectively, and can be assigned to the oxygen in CO, C=O and OC=O groups [39]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the deconvoluted XPS C1s spectrum of NFZ-GQDs exhibited three peaks that correspond to the carbon atoms in different functional groups: the nonoxygenated C=C/CC bond (284.5 eV), the CO bond of epoxy and hydroxyl (285.9 eV) and the carboxyl OC=O (288.4 eV). The peak at 283.3 eV can be assigned to the C-M bond [41], which indicates that a bond between C and Zn has formed during the formation of the nanofibers. In the spectrum of NFZ-rGO, deconvolution also yielded three peaks with binding energies at 284.1 eV (CC/C=C), 285.2 eV (C–O), and 287.6 eV (CO) [39]. These results demonstrate that graphene-based nanomaterials were indeed successfully grafted with NFZ, consistent with the XRD results.

Before using the modified electrodes for glucose monitoring, the electrical properties of the nanomaterials were evaluated by impedance spectroscopy. Fig. 3 shows the electrical resistance curves, extracted from the real part of impedance data, as a function of frequency, for each sensing unit in PBS (0.1 mol L− 1, pH = 7). The change in the resistance values in the region between 100 Hz and 10 kHz provides a tool to verify the effect of film coating on the electrical responses of IDEs [33]. As can be seen, the electrical resistance of the nanocomposites decreased in comparison with that of pure ZnO nanofibers. Such behavior arises due to the synergistic effect between NFZ and graphene-based nanomaterials, and it can be mainly attributed to their good electrical conductivity and capability to increase the concentration of available charge carriers [11,12].

The attachment of GOx enzyme to the modified IDEs was further confirmed by impedance measurements and the obtained data are displayed in Fig. 4 a-c in the form of Nyquist plots. As can be seen, when the enzyme was attached onto the modified electrodes, a significantly increased semicircle could be observed. Such result evidences that the GOx was successfully immobilized, once the non-conductive nature of the enzyme hinders the electron transfer [42]. The Nyquist plots of the electrodes modified with GOx were fitted with the equivalent circuits shown in Fig. 4d and the respective fitting values are shown in Table 1 . In this work, the theoretical model for impedance was based on an equivalent electrical circuit consisting of a combination between resistors and capacitors. The elements R1 and R2 represent the electrical resistance regarding the contact of the interdigitated electrode with the nanomaterial and the electrical resistance of the inorganic phase, respectively. The C1 element was considered to model the double-layer capacitance. The R3 and C2 elements were considered to adjust the impedance data of the nanocomposites and correspond to the resistance of the graphene derivatives and to the charge transfer across the film, respectively [43,44]. The phase constant elements (QPE1 and QPE2) were included to improve the fitting process.

To evaluate the analytical performance of the proposed biosensors, the resistance changes of the sensing units were recorded by analyzing a set of glucose concentrations. As displayed in the insets of Fig. 5 , the semicircle domain increases upon the addition of different glucose concentrations. The electrical resistance increase might be a result of the augmented amount of glucose and/or the products from the enzymatic reaction (reaction 1) on the electrode surface, which increases the barrier for direct current flow along the electrode. The same behavior was observed by Balakrishnan et al. during the detection of glucose using (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) and GOx [45]. In addition, Fig. 5 also shows the linear dependence between the resistance (obtained from fitting the experimental data) and the glucose concentration in the range of 0.1 to 6 mM, which covers the salivary glucose levels found in healthy and diabetic patients [46,47]. The estimated LOD values (defined as 3 σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank impedance signal (three replicates) and S is the slope of the related calibration curve) were found to be 32 and 14 μM for NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx, respectively. It should also be noted that a linear dependence was not observed for the sensing platform based on pristine ZnO nanofibers (data not shown), which demonstrates the effectiveness of developing hybrid nanostructures for biosensing applications.
(1)
glucose
+
O 2
→ GOx
gluconic
acid
+
H 2
O 2

Analytical performances obtained in this work were compared with results reported in the literature, and are shown in Table 2 . As can be seen, our developed biosensors exhibit similar or better analytical characteristics than recently reported works, indicating that the proposed approach has potential to be employed in the design of biosensors with superior performance.

Saliva is considered to be the most suitable alternative compared to blood for non-invasive diabetes monitoring because of easy sample collection from the patients [6]. More importantly, the strong correlation between blood glucose and salivary glucose concentrations is considered to be well established for both non-diabetic and diabetic patients [46,47]. Therefore, the applicability of the proposed biosensors was evaluated to detect glucose in artificial saliva samples. The impedance data were obtained for artificial saliva samples spiked with different concentrations of glucose (0.1, 2 and 6 mM). Table 3 shows the obtained recovered percentages, which ranged from 87.3% to 106.8%. Moreover, the biosensors also showed a stability with a signal variation of approximately 7 and 9% for NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx, respectively, after seven successive measurements carried out using the same electrode. When three nominally identical electrodes were used, the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 9.7 and 11.3% for NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx, respectively. The good analytical performance obtained here can be ascribed to a combination of three important features: (i) high surface area and high electrical conductivity of the nanomaterials employed in the design of the biosensors, (ii) synergistic effect between the ceramic nanofibers and graphene-based nanomaterials and (iii) high loading capacity for the modified-electrode surfaces using electrospun nanofibers, which allows the immobilization of a relatively large amount of glucose oxidase.

To improve the sensing performance and extend the application fields, biosensors have been incorporated into arrays, leading to the development of bioelectronic tongues [26,53–55]. Unlike specific sensors, the multisensor system uses advanced mathematical procedures for signal processing based on pattern recognition and/or multivariate analysis, which is capable to extract meaningful data from the complex readings [53,56]. The main advantage of bioelectronic tongues lies in their ability to respond reproducibly to a wide number of compounds, allowing the monitoring of different analytes in a complex media, without performing any pretreatment, by counterbalancing any interference or matrix effect [26,53].

Therefore, in order to represent the dissimilarity between glucose concentrations, the impedance data of the three sensing units (NFZ@GOx, NFZ-GQDs@GOx and NFZ-rGO@GOx) were treated with PCA technique, as shown in Fig. 6 . The discrimination ability was quantized by the silhouette coefficient (S), whose values range between −1 and 1 [57]. In short, S values between 0.71 and 1.0 mean strong classification, whereas values close to zero have no relevance for discrimination, and negative values hinder distinction [16]. The obtained values of S were 0.73, which means strong discrimination between different glucose concentrations (Fig. 6a) and glucose and possible interfering substances (Fig. 6b). As shown in Fig. 6a, there is a correlation between glucose concentration and PC1 (as the concentration increases, the samples tend to locate to the right side of x-axis) clearly indicating that the array could discriminate different glucose solutions. As previously mentioned, such concentration range covers the glucose levels found in healthy and diabetic patients.

In Fig. 6b, PC1 separated the samples into two clustered groups, i.e., glucose samples and a group of interfering compounds. Therefore, this result demonstrates that the enzymatic sensor array can be selective for glucose, which discrimination capability does not suffer from interference of other analytes that may be present in saliva [46,58–60]. While the glucose solutions with different concentrations were again separated by the PC1 values, the ascorbic acid (AA) and inorganic salts were separated by PC2. Moreover, the data variance of 93.8% and 79% concentrated in PC1 + PC2 shows that the results are highly correlated and the statistical technique was quite relevant for the desired analysis.

We fabricated biosensors by functionalizing ZnO nanofibers with graphene-based nanomaterials and glucose oxidase to specifically detect glucose using impedance spectroscopy. Specifically, ZnO nanofibers were successfully fabricated by electrospinning and combined with GQDs and rGO to produce suitable platforms for GOx immobilization. The combination of nanofibers with GQDs and rGO resulted in enhanced charge transfer, leading to improved electrical properties ascribed to the high surface area of the nanocomposites and to the synergistic effect between the nanomaterials. Moreover, the prepared biosensors were applied to determine glucose in small volumes of artificial saliva with satisfactory results. In addition, the sensing units were employed to build a bioelectronic tongue for the successful discrimination of different glucose levels and for the determination of glucose in the presence of interfering compounds generally present in human saliva. Therefore, the experimental procedure outlined here could be followed for other biomarkers diluted in artificial saliva to test the sensor's effectiveness in biomarker detection associated to varied diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo (FAPESP) (2016/23793-4, 2017/10582-8, 2017/12174-4, 2018/22214-6), Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), MCTI-SisNano (CNPq/402.287/2013-4), and Rede Agronano (EMBRAPA) from Brazil. The authors also thank the Laboratory of Structural Characterization (LCE/DEMa/UFSCar) for the microscopy facilities and Dr.Valmor Mastelaro (IFSC-USP) for the XPS measurements.
